scenario_blurb: Stage III/IV melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation. Treated with combination BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) and/or checkpoint immunotherapy (nivolumab plus ipilimumab, pembrolizumab). Some with brain metastases. Include LDH levels.

events.text: Patient is a 67-year-old Caucasian male with a history of multiple prior non-melanoma skin cancers and significant cumulative sun exposure, presenting with a bleeding nodular lesion on his right scalp.

At age 67, patient was diagnosed with nodular melanoma (ICD-O morphology 8721/3) of the skin of the head (ICD-O topography C44.3), staged as Stage IV (T4bN3M1c) with synchronous lung and liver metastases.

At age 67, punch biopsy followed by wide excisional biopsy of the right scalp lesion revealed nodular melanoma with Breslow thickness of 6.2 mm, Clark level V, ulceration present, mitotic rate 12/mm2, extensive lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion identified, and microsatellites present.
At age 67, wide local excision with 2 cm margins was performed on the right scalp with rotation flap closure for local disease control despite known metastatic disease.
At age 67, palpable right cervical lymph node fine needle aspiration confirmed metastatic melanoma, positive for HMB-45 and SOX10.

At age 67, staging CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 2.3 cm), three hepatic lesions (largest 3.8 cm in the right lobe), and bulky right cervical lymphadenopathy measuring 4.1 cm.
At age 67, MRI of the brain with gadolinium showed two enhancing lesions in the right frontal lobe (1.8 cm) and left parietal lobe (1.2 cm) consistent with brain metastases.

At age 67, comprehensive genomic profiling of the primary tumor identified BRAF V600E mutation (allele frequency 55%), NRAS wild-type, TERT promoter mutation (C228T), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, TP53 R248W mutation, high tumor mutational burden of 28 mutations per megabase, and PD-L1 combined positive score of 15.
At age 67, serum LDH was significantly elevated at 485 U/L, and the patient was classified as Stage IV M1d due to brain metastases with an ECOG performance status of 1.
At age 67, stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) was delivered to the two brain metastases: 20 Gy in 1 fraction to the right frontal lesion and 22 Gy in 1 fraction to the left parietal lesion.
At age 67, combination ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks was initiated for treatment of metastatic melanoma with brain metastases given high PD-L1 and high TMB.

At age 67, after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, patient developed grade 3 immune-related colitis with bloody diarrhea (8-10 stools/day), requiring hospitalization, IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg, and discontinuation of ipilimumab.
At age 67, colitis resolved to grade 1 over 3 weeks with steroid taper, and the decision was made to continue nivolumab monotherapy.
At age 67, nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks was continued as maintenance immunotherapy after ipilimumab discontinuation.
At age 67, restaging CT at 12 weeks showed mixed response with reduction in pulmonary nodules but stable hepatic lesions, assessed as stable disease per RECIST 1.1.
At age 68, MRI brain at 12 weeks post-SRS showed treatment effect with decreased enhancement of both lesions and no new intracranial lesions.
At age 68, LDH trended down to 342 U/L suggesting partial biochemical response to therapy.
At age 68, restaging PET/CT at 6 months showed continued partial response in pulmonary metastases and stable hepatic disease with decreased FDG uptake.
At age 68, patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 arthralgias attributed to nivolumab, managed symptomatically without dose modification.
At age 68, restaging CT at 9 months showed progression of hepatic metastases with new lesion in left hepatic lobe despite stable pulmonary disease.
At age 68, given hepatic progression on nivolumab monotherapy with confirmed BRAF V600E mutation, decision was made to switch to BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy, and LDH rose to 410 U/L.
At age 68, dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg daily was initiated as second-line therapy for progressive BRAF V600E melanoma.
At age 68, restaging CT at 8 weeks on dabrafenib/trametinib showed significant response with greater than 50% reduction in hepatic metastases and continued stability of pulmonary disease.

documents.text:
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

SPECIMEN ID: SP-24-08471

TYPE OF SPECIMEN:
A. Punch biopsy, right scalp lesion
B. Wide excisional biopsy, right scalp lesion

CLINICAL HISTORY:
67-year-old male with bleeding nodular lesion on right scalp. History of multiple prior non-melanoma skin cancers. Significant cumulative sun exposure.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS:

Specimen A (Punch biopsy, right scalp):
Sections show a dome-shaped dermal nodule composed of sheets of epithelioid and focally spindled melanocytes with prominent nucleoli, abundant mitotic figures, and extensive pigmentation. The tumor invades through the full thickness of the dermis into subcutaneous fat. Ulceration of the overlying epidermis is identified. There is no appreciable junctional component.

Diagnosis: MALIGNANT MELANOMA, NODULAR TYPE

Specimen B (Wide excisional biopsy, right scalp):
Sections confirm a nodular melanoma centered in the dermis and extending into the subcutaneous adipose tissue.

- Breslow thickness: 6.2 mm
- Clark level: V (invasion into subcutaneous fat)
- Ulceration: Present
- Mitotic rate: 12 mitoses per mm2
- Growth pattern: Nodular
- Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes: Non-brisk
- Lymphovascular invasion: Extensive, identified in multiple sections
- Perineural invasion: Present, involving a small-caliber dermal nerve
- Microsatellites: Present (discrete tumor deposit >0.1 mm identified in dermis/subcutis separate from main tumor mass)
- Regression: Absent
- Cell type: Predominantly epithelioid with focal spindle cell areas
- Margins: Peripheral margins clear by 1.4 cm; deep margin clear by 0.3 cm

pT4b (ulcerated, >4.0 mm Breslow thickness)

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
Immunohistochemistry performed on specimen A:
- S-100: Positive (diffuse, strong)
- HMB-45: Positive (patchy)
- Melan-A/MART-1: Positive (patchy)
- SOX10: Positive (diffuse, strong nuclear)
- Ki-67 proliferation index: Approximately 45%

GROSS PATHOLOGY:
Specimen A: A 0.4 cm diameter punch biopsy of skin with a 0.9 cm pigmented nodular lesion. Entirely submitted.

Specimen B: An ellipse of skin measuring 8.2 x 5.1 x 1.8 cm with a central pigmented, partially ulcerated nodular lesion measuring 3.2 x 2.8 cm. The lesion is dark brown to black with surface crusting. Margins inked: superior (blue), inferior (green), deep (black). Serially sectioned and entirely submitted in cassettes B1-B14.

CYTOPATHOLOGY REPORT

SPECIMEN ID: CY-24-03215

TYPE OF SPECIMEN:
Fine needle aspiration, right cervical lymph node (level II), ultrasound-guided

CLINICAL HISTORY:
67-year-old male with recently diagnosed nodular melanoma of the right scalp (Breslow 6.2 mm). Palpable right cervical lymphadenopathy. FNA performed for staging.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS:
The aspirate is moderately cellular and consists of loosely cohesive groups and isolated large epithelioid cells with eccentrically placed nuclei, prominent cherry-red nucleoli, and moderate amounts of cytoplasm containing fine melanin pigment granules. Scattered bi- and multinucleated tumor giant cells are identified. Background shows mature lymphocytes and occasional tingible body macrophages. No necrosis is identified.

Diagnosis: POSITIVE FOR METASTATIC MALIGNANT MELANOMA

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
Cell block immunohistochemistry:
- HMB-45: Positive (strong, diffuse cytoplasmic)
- SOX10: Positive (strong, diffuse nuclear)
- S-100: Positive (strong, diffuse)
- Melan-A/MART-1: Positive (moderate, cytoplasmic)
- Cytokeratin AE1/AE3: Negative
- CD45/LCA: Negative in tumor cells; positive in background lymphocytes

GROSS PATHOLOGY:
Ultrasound-guided FNA of a 4.1 cm hypoechoic right level II cervical lymph node using a 22-gauge needle. Three passes performed. Direct smears prepared and stained with Diff-Quik and Papanicolaou stain. Residual material submitted for cell block preparation. Preliminary adequacy assessment: adequate.

CT CHEST, ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Newly diagnosed melanoma of the scalp. Staging evaluation. Known cervical lymphadenopathy.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following intravenous administration of 100 mL iohexol (Omnipaque 350). Axial images reconstructed at 1.25 mm and 5 mm intervals with coronal and sagittal reformats.

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
- Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules are identified, the largest measuring 2.3 cm in the right lower lobe (series 4, image 187). Additional nodules include: 1.8 cm left lower lobe, 1.4 cm right upper lobe, 1.1 cm left upper lobe, and at least 6 smaller subcentimeter nodules bilaterally ranging from 0.4 to 0.8 cm.
- No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
- Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion.
- Mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes are within normal limits.
- Bulky right cervical lymphadenopathy noted on the superior images, with a conglomerate nodal mass measuring approximately 4.1 x 3.3 cm at level II. Additional right level III node measuring 1.8 cm.

Abdomen:
- Liver: Three discrete hepatic lesions are identified. The largest measures 3.8 x 3.2 cm in segment VI/VII of the right hepatic lobe with peripheral enhancement and central hypoattenuation. Additional lesions measure 2.1 cm in segment IVa and 1.5 cm in segment VIII. These are suspicious for metastatic disease.
- Spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys: Unremarkable.
- No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.
- Gallbladder is normal. No intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.

Pelvis:
- Urinary bladder is normal. Prostate is mildly enlarged.
- No pelvic lymphadenopathy.
- No free fluid.

Musculoskeletal:
- No suspicious osseous lesions.
- Mild degenerative changes of the thoracolumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:
1. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest 2.3 cm in the right lower lobe, highly suspicious for pulmonary metastases in the setting of newly diagnosed melanoma.
2. Three hepatic lesions (largest 3.8 cm, right hepatic lobe) with enhancement pattern consistent with metastatic disease.
3. Bulky right cervical lymphadenopathy (4.1 cm) and additional right level III adenopathy (1.8 cm), consistent with nodal metastatic disease.
4. No osseous metastatic disease identified.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Newly diagnosed Stage IV melanoma with pulmonary and hepatic metastases. Screening for intracranial metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3T scanner. Pre- and post-gadolinium (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg IV) T1-weighted, T2-weighted, FLAIR, diffusion-weighted, and susceptibility-weighted sequences obtained.

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS:

Brain Parenchyma:
- Right frontal lobe: A 1.8 x 1.5 cm well-circumscribed, round, homogeneously enhancing intra-axial lesion is identified in the right superior frontal gyrus. The lesion demonstrates T1 hyperintensity on pre-contrast images suggesting intralesional hemorrhage or melanin content. Surrounding vasogenic edema extends approximately 1.5 cm into the adjacent white matter. Mild local mass effect on the right lateral ventricle without midline shift.
- Left parietal lobe: A 1.2 x 1.0 cm enhancing lesion is identified in the left postcentral gyrus with similar T1 hyperintensity and mild surrounding edema. No significant mass effect.
- No additional enhancing lesions identified.
- No evidence of leptomeningeal enhancement.
- No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction.

Ventricles and Extra-axial Spaces:
- Ventricles are symmetric and normal in caliber.
- No hydrocephalus.
- Mild generalized cortical atrophy appropriate for age.

Midline Structures:
- No midline shift.
- Pituitary gland is normal.

Posterior Fossa:
- Cerebellum and brainstem are normal in appearance.
- No posterior fossa lesions identified.

Calvarium and Scalp:
- Post-surgical changes noted in the right parietal scalp consistent with recent excision site.
- No calvarial metastatic lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Two enhancing brain metastases: 1.8 cm right frontal lobe (superior frontal gyrus) and 1.2 cm left parietal lobe (postcentral gyrus). Both demonstrate T1 hyperintensity suggesting melanin content or hemorrhage.
2. Mild surrounding vasogenic edema, right greater than left, without significant mass effect or midline shift.
3. No leptomeningeal disease.
4. Findings reclassify disease as M1d per AJCC 8th edition.

COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

SPECIMEN INFORMATION:
Specimen type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
Specimen source: Right scalp, wide excisional biopsy
Tumor type: Melanoma, nodular type
Tumor cellularity: 70%
Specimen adequacy: Adequate for analysis

ASSAY INFORMATION:
Panel: 523-gene comprehensive genomic profiling panel
Sequencing platform: Hybrid capture-based next-generation sequencing
Median exon coverage: 782x
Genomic coverage: 1.8 Mb coding region

DIAGNOSIS:
Nodular melanoma, right scalp (ICD-O: 8721/3, C44.3)
Stage IV (T4bN3M1c), AJCC 8th edition

GENOMIC FINDINGS:

Tier I - Clinically Actionable Alterations:

1. BRAF V600E (c.1799T>A)
   Variant allele frequency: 55%
   Clinical significance: Activating mutation. Sensitive to BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib, encorafenib) and BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations (dabrafenib/trametinib, encorafenib/binimetinib). FDA-approved targeted therapies available for BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma.

2. Tumor Mutational Burden: 28 mutations/Mb (HIGH)
   Clinical significance: High TMB is associated with increased likelihood of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab FDA-approved for TMB-High solid tumors).

3. PD-L1 Combined Positive Score (CPS): 15
   Method: Immunohistochemistry (22C3 pharmDx assay)
   Clinical significance: Positive. Supports consideration of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

Tier II - Potentially Actionable Alterations:

4. CDKN2A homozygous deletion (p16/INK4a and p14/ARF)
   Clinical significance: Loss of cell cycle regulation. Associated with poorer prognosis in melanoma. CDK4/6 inhibitors under investigation.

5. TERT promoter mutation (C228T; chr5:1,295,228 C>T)
   Clinical significance: Gain-of-function promoter mutation leading to increased telomerase expression. One of the most frequent mutations in melanoma. Associated with aggressive disease.

Tier III - Pathogenic Alterations, Limited Actionability:

6. TP53 R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant allele frequency: 38%
   Clinical significance: Known hotspot loss-of-function mutation. Associated with impaired DNA damage response.

Negative Findings (Clinically Relevant):
- NRAS: Wild-type (codons 12, 13, 61)
- KIT: Wild-type (exons 9, 11, 13, 17)
- NF1: Wild-type
- MAP2K1 (MEK1): Wild-type
- CTNNB1: Wild-type

Microsatellite status: Microsatellite Stable (MSS)

VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE:
- ARID1A Q1334H (c.4002G>T), VAF 12%
- FAT1 E2467K (c.7399G>A), VAF 18%
- NOTCH2 P2081S (c.6241C>T), VAF 15%
- ERCC2 D312N (c.934G>A), VAF 22%

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Newly diagnosed metastatic melanoma with brain metastases, referred for medical oncology evaluation and treatment planning.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 67-year-old Caucasian gentleman who presented approximately 5 weeks ago with a bleeding, enlarging nodular lesion on his right scalp that had been present for an estimated 4-6 months. The patient reports the lesion started as a small dark spot that gradually became raised and began to bleed with minor trauma. He initially attributed it to trauma from his hat. Punch biopsy followed by wide excisional biopsy confirmed nodular melanoma, Breslow thickness 6.2 mm, Clark level V, with ulceration, extensive LVI, perineural invasion, and microsatellites. Wide local excision with 2 cm margins and rotation flap closure has been completed.

FNA of a palpable right cervical node confirmed metastatic melanoma (HMB-45+, SOX10+). Staging CT CAP demonstrated multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 2.3 cm RLL), three hepatic lesions (largest 3.8 cm right lobe), and bulky right cervical lymphadenopathy (4.1 cm). MRI brain revealed two enhancing brain metastases: 1.8 cm right frontal and 1.2 cm left parietal, both with T1 hyperintensity suggesting melanin content.

Comprehensive genomic profiling returned today with BRAF V600E mutation (VAF 55%), high TMB at 28 mut/Mb, PD-L1 CPS of 15, TERT promoter mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and TP53 R248W. NRAS is wild-type.

Serum LDH drawn today is significantly elevated at 485 U/L (normal 120-246). Per AJCC 8th edition, disease is reclassified as Stage IV M1d given brain metastases.

The patient reports mild right-sided headaches over the past 2-3 weeks, intermittent, not severe, no nausea or vomiting. He reports moderate fatigue and approximately 8-pound weight loss over 3 months. He continues to have mild wound discomfort at the scalp excision site. No other focal neurological symptoms. No fevers, night sweats. Appetite is decreased.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue, weight loss (8 lbs over 3 months), decreased appetite. No fevers or night sweats.
HEENT: Mild intermittent right-sided headache. No vision changes, diplopia.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or peripheral edema.
GI: No nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or constipation.
Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. Nocturia x2 at baseline.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or muscle weakness.
Neurological: Mild headache as above. No focal weakness, numbness, seizures, speech difficulty.
Skin: Scalp surgical site healing well. No new concerning lesions noted by patient.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Multiple prior non-melanoma skin cancers (5+ basal cell carcinomas, 2 squamous cell carcinomas of skin, all excised)
2. Hypertension, well-controlled
3. Hyperlipidemia
4. Benign prostatic hyperplasia
5. Osteoarthritis, bilateral knees
6. Remote history of atrial fibrillation, cardioverted, currently in sinus rhythm, on anticoagulation

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired commercial fisherman. Significant cumulative sun exposure over 40+ year career. Former smoker, 20 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago. Occasional alcohol, 2-3 beers per week. Lives with wife. Two adult children. Independent with all ADLs. ECOG performance status 1.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: prostate cancer, age 72, deceased age 81. Mother: breast cancer, age 65, deceased age 78. No family history of melanoma. Sister alive, age 64, healthy.

ALLERGIES:
Sulfonamides - rash

MEDICATIONS:
1. Lisinopril 20 mg daily
2. Atorvastatin 40 mg daily
3. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily
4. Apixaban 5 mg twice daily
5. Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed
6. Dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily (started 3 days ago for cerebral edema)

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 138/82, HR 76, Temp 97.9 F, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 187 lbs, Height 5'11", BMI 26.1
General: Alert, oriented, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Appears stated age.
HEENT: Scalp surgical site on right parietal/temporal area with rotation flap, well-healed, no erythema or drainage. PERRL, EOMI. Oropharynx clear.
Neck: Right cervical lymphadenopathy palpable at level II, approximately 4 cm, firm, non-tender, fixed. No left cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge palpable 3 cm below right costal margin. No splenomegaly.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No cyanosis or clubbing.
Neurological: Alert, oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 all extremities. Sensation intact. Gait normal. No pronator drift. Romberg negative.
Skin: Full-body skin exam performed. No additional suspicious pigmented lesions identified. Multiple scars from prior skin cancer excisions on bilateral forearms, nose, left ear.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 8.7 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, Platelets 267 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.04 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL
AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 128 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL
Albumin 3.6 g/dL
LDH 485 U/L (elevated)
TSH 2.14 mIU/L
Glucose 142 mg/dL (likely steroid effect)

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT CAP: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 2.3 cm RLL), three hepatic lesions (largest 3.8 cm right lobe), bulky right cervical adenopathy 4.1 cm.
MRI Brain: Two enhancing brain metastases - 1.8 cm right frontal, 1.2 cm left parietal.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
67-year-old male with newly diagnosed Stage IV (M1d) BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma with metastases to lungs, liver, cervical lymph nodes, and brain. High TMB (28 mut/Mb), PD-L1 CPS 15. ECOG PS 1. Elevated LDH at 485.

1. BRAIN METASTASES: Two limited brain metastases amenable to stereotactic radiosurgery. Refer to radiation oncology for SRS consultation. Given limited number and size, SRS preferred over whole brain radiation to preserve neurocognitive function. Continue dexamethasone 4 mg BID pending SRS.

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY: Despite BRAF V600E positivity, given high TMB, PD-L1 CPS 15, and evidence supporting immunotherapy in the frontline setting for brain metastases, recommend combination ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 cycles as first-line therapy, followed by nivo maintenance. The CheckMate 204 data supports intracranial activity with ipi/nivo. BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib) reserved for second-line if progression.

3. ANTICOAGULATION: Discussed with patient the risk of intracranial hemorrhage in the setting of brain metastases with melanin/hemorrhagic features. Given SRS planned, will hold apixaban 3 days prior and resume 1 week post-SRS per radiation oncology guidance.

4. LABS: Check CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH at each visit. Monitor LFTs closely given hepatic metastatic burden.

5. SUPPORTIVE CARE: Continue dexamethasone with plan to taper after SRS. PPI started for steroid prophylaxis. Glucose monitoring given steroid-induced hyperglycemia.

6. FOLLOW-UP: Return in 2 weeks after SRS for cycle 1 of ipi/nivo. Restaging scans at 12 weeks.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up after hospitalization for immune-related colitis.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 67-year-old male with Stage IV (M1d) BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma with metastases to lungs, liver, cervical lymph nodes, and brain (post-SRS) who presents for follow-up. He completed 2 cycles of ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg. After cycle 2, he developed grade 3 immune-related colitis with bloody diarrhea (8-10 stools per day), was hospitalized for 5 days, treated with IV Solu-Medrol 2 mg/kg with good response, and ipilimumab was permanently discontinued.

He was discharged on oral prednisone 60 mg with taper. Over the past 3 weeks, the taper has proceeded well: 60 mg x 1 week, 40 mg x 1 week, currently on 20 mg with plan to continue tapering by 10 mg per week. Diarrhea has resolved from grade 3 to grade 1 -- he reports 3-4 formed to semi-formed stools daily, no blood, no abdominal cramping. He has been tolerating a regular diet. Weight is stable since hospital discharge.

He reports mild cushingoid symptoms from steroids including facial puffiness, increased appetite, and difficulty sleeping. Glucose has been running 150-180s on home monitoring. No fevers, no recurrent bloody stools.

Given resolution of colitis and need to maintain immunotherapy, the plan is to continue nivolumab monotherapy at the flat dose of 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. The combination ipi/nivo will not be rechallenged.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improved from hospitalization. No fevers, no night sweats. Appetite increased (likely steroid effect).
GI: 3-4 stools daily, formed to semi-formed. No blood, no mucus, no cramping. Tolerating regular diet.
Endocrine: Facial puffiness, insomnia on steroids. Home glucoses 150-180.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 142/86, HR 80, Temp 98.2 F, RR 14, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 189 lbs
General: Alert, oriented, cushingoid facies, mild moon face. No acute distress.
HEENT: Scalp surgical site healed. Right cervical lymphadenopathy still palpable but subjectively slightly smaller, approximately 3.5 cm.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normoactive bowel sounds. Liver edge palpable 2-3 cm below costal margin.
Extremities: No edema. Trace ankle edema on right.
Skin: No rash. No new lesions.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 12.4 x10^9/L (elevated, steroid effect), Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Platelets 298 x10^9/L
Creatinine 0.98 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL
AST 35 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
Albumin 3.3 g/dL (low, post-colitis)
LDH 438 U/L (slightly decreased from 485, but still elevated)
TSH 1.88 mIU/L
Glucose 168 mg/dL (steroid effect)
CRP 8.2 mg/L (trending down from 45 at hospitalization)

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
67-year-old male with Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma, now status post 2 cycles ipi/nivo complicated by grade 3 immune-related colitis, resolving on steroid taper.

1. IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS: Grade 3, now resolved to grade 1. Continue prednisone taper: 20 mg x 1 week, then 10 mg x 1 week, then 5 mg x 1 week, then discontinue. If any recurrence of bloody diarrhea, hold taper and call clinic.

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY: Permanently discontinue ipilimumab given grade 3 colitis. Transition to nivolumab monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. First cycle of nivo monotherapy to be administered today given colitis resolved to grade 1 and patient on tapering steroids. Literature supports continuing anti-PD-1 monotherapy after irAE from anti-CTLA-4.

3. STEROID MANAGEMENT: Taper as above. Omeprazole 20 mg daily for GI prophylaxis while on steroids. Home glucose monitoring; if persistently >200, will start metformin.

4. DISEASE MONITORING: LDH trended slightly down to 438 from 485. Restaging CT CAP and MRI brain scheduled at 12 weeks from cycle 1 of ipi/nivo.

5. ANTICOAGULATION: Apixaban 5 mg BID resumed 1 week post-SRS. Continue.

6. RETURN: 4 weeks for next cycle of nivolumab. Check CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH prior to each infusion.

CT CHEST, ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Metastatic melanoma (BRAF V600E, Stage IV M1d). Restaging at 12 weeks on immunotherapy (ipilimumab/nivolumab x2 cycles, then nivolumab monotherapy). Evaluate treatment response.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following intravenous administration of 100 mL iohexol. Axial images reconstructed at 1.25 mm and 5 mm intervals with coronal and sagittal reformats.

COMPARISON: CT CAP from approximately 12 weeks prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
- Bilateral pulmonary nodules have decreased in size and number. The dominant right lower lobe nodule now measures 1.6 cm (previously 2.3 cm). Left lower lobe nodule measures 1.2 cm (previously 1.8 cm). Right upper lobe nodule measures 0.8 cm (previously 1.4 cm). Left upper lobe nodule stable at 1.0 cm (previously 1.1 cm). Several previously identified subcentimeter nodules are no longer visualized.
- No new pulmonary nodules.
- No pleural effusion.
- Heart size normal. No pericardial effusion.
- Right cervical lymphadenopathy at level II has decreased, now measuring 2.8 x 2.2 cm (previously 4.1 x 3.3 cm). Right level III node now 1.2 cm (previously 1.8 cm).

Abdomen:
- Liver: Three hepatic lesions remain visible. The dominant right hepatic lobe lesion (segment VI/VII) measures 3.6 x 3.0 cm (previously 3.8 x 3.2 cm), minimally decreased. Segment IVa lesion stable at 2.0 cm (previously 2.1 cm). Segment VIII lesion stable at 1.5 cm. Enhancement pattern is unchanged.
- No new hepatic lesions.
- Spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys: Unremarkable.
- No ascites.
- No new retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis:
- Unremarkable. No new findings.

Musculoskeletal:
- No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Mixed response: Interval decrease in bilateral pulmonary nodules and cervical lymphadenopathy, consistent with partial response in these sites.
2. Hepatic metastases essentially stable, with only minimal size decrease in the dominant lesion.
3. Overall assessment per RECIST 1.1: Stable disease (sum of target lesions decreased by approximately 18%, not meeting threshold for partial response).
4. No new sites of disease.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Metastatic melanoma with known brain metastases treated with stereotactic radiosurgery approximately 12 weeks ago. Surveillance imaging.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3T scanner. Pre- and post-gadolinium T1-weighted, T2-weighted, FLAIR, diffusion-weighted, and susceptibility-weighted sequences obtained.

COMPARISON: MRI brain from approximately 14 weeks prior (pre-SRS).

FINDINGS:

Brain Parenchyma:
- Right frontal lobe: The previously treated metastasis in the right superior frontal gyrus now measures 1.3 x 1.1 cm (previously 1.8 x 1.5 cm). There is decreased homogeneous enhancement with development of a thin peripheral ring of enhancement, consistent with treatment effect. Surrounding edema has decreased. T1 hyperintensity persists but is less prominent. Mild susceptibility artifact from hemosiderin deposition.
- Left parietal lobe: The previously treated lesion in the left postcentral gyrus now measures 0.7 x 0.6 cm (previously 1.2 x 1.0 cm). Minimal residual enhancement. Surrounding edema has resolved. Punctate susceptibility artifact noted.
- No new enhancing lesions identified on post-gadolinium sequences.
- No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction or tumor progression.
- No leptomeningeal enhancement.

Ventricles and Extra-axial Spaces:
- Ventricles symmetric and normal in caliber.
- No hydrocephalus.

Midline Structures:
- No midline shift.

Posterior Fossa:
- No new posterior fossa lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Interval decrease in size and enhancement of both treated brain metastases, consistent with favorable treatment response to stereotactic radiosurgery.
2. Right frontal lesion: 1.3 cm (previously 1.8 cm) with treatment effect pattern.
3. Left parietal lesion: 0.7 cm (previously 1.2 cm) with near-complete response.
4. No new intracranial metastatic disease.
5. No leptomeningeal disease.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up on nivolumab monotherapy, review of restaging scans.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 68-year-old male with Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma with metastases to lungs, liver, cervical nodes, and brain (post-SRS), currently receiving nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks (cycle 4 of monotherapy today). Prior treatment: 2 cycles of ipi/nivo complicated by grade 3 immune-related colitis requiring hospitalization and permanent ipi discontinuation. Steroids have been fully tapered off for approximately 4 weeks without recurrence of colitis.

Restaging imaging was obtained this week:
- CT CAP showed mixed response with decrease in pulmonary nodules and cervical lymphadenopathy but stable hepatic lesions. Overall stable disease by RECIST 1.1.
- MRI brain showed favorable response to SRS with decrease in both brain metastases and no new intracranial disease.

LDH has trended down from 485 to 342 U/L, suggesting partial biochemical response.

The patient feels well. Energy has improved since completing steroid taper. He reports mild fatigue, rated 3/10 on most days. No headaches. Bowel function has fully normalized -- 1-2 formed stools daily. Appetite is good and he has regained 3 lbs of weight. He went fishing last weekend for the first time since diagnosis.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Mild fatigue. No fevers, night sweats. Weight stable.
GI: Normal bowel function, no diarrhea.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal deficits.
Skin: No new concerning lesions.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 132/78, HR 72, Temp 98.0 F, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, Weight 192 lbs
General: Alert, well-appearing. Cushingoid features have resolved. ECOG PS 1.
HEENT: Scalp healed. Right cervical adenopathy palpable but smaller, approximately 2.5 cm.
Chest: Clear bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Liver edge palpable 2 cm below costal margin.
Neurological: Non-focal. Cranial nerves intact.
Skin: Full-body exam: No new suspicious lesions.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, Platelets 245 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.02 mg/dL, BUN 16 mg/dL
AST 38 U/L, ALT 33 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 115 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL
Albumin 3.8 g/dL
LDH 342 U/L (decreased from 438, and from initial 485)
TSH 2.42 mIU/L
Free T4 1.1 ng/dL
Glucose 108 mg/dL (normalized off steroids)

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
68-year-old male with Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma showing mixed response on nivolumab monotherapy: brain metastases responding to SRS, pulmonary disease improving, hepatic disease stable.

1. SYSTEMIC THERAPY: Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. Administer cycle 4 today. Hepatic lesions are stable rather than progressing, and the overall disease trajectory is favorable with declining LDH. Will continue current therapy with close monitoring.

2. DISEASE MONITORING: LDH 342, down from 485 at diagnosis, a favorable biochemical trend. Brain metastases responding well to SRS. Continue MRI brain every 3 months.

3. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EFFECTS: Monitor for thyroid dysfunction (TSH normal today), hepatitis (LFTs stable), pneumonitis, and recurrent colitis. Continue TSH at each cycle.

4. NEXT RESTAGING: PET/CT in approximately 6 weeks (at the 6-month mark from initiation of therapy) to better characterize metabolic response of hepatic lesions.

5. RETURN: 4 weeks for nivolumab cycle 5.

PET/CT WITH 18F-FDG

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma on nivolumab monotherapy. 6-month restaging. Evaluate metabolic response, particularly hepatic metastases stable by CT RECIST.

TECHNIQUE:
18F-FDG PET/CT performed approximately 60 minutes after IV administration of 12.3 mCi of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose. Blood glucose at time of injection: 102 mg/dL. Low-dose CT obtained for attenuation correction and anatomical correlation. Images acquired from skull vertex to mid-thigh.

COMPARISON: CT CAP from approximately 6 weeks prior. No prior PET/CT available.

FINDINGS:

Head and Neck:
- Post-surgical changes right scalp without FDG-avid disease.
- Right cervical level II lymphadenopathy, now measuring approximately 2.2 cm, with mild residual FDG uptake (SUVmax 3.4). This has decreased in size compared to prior CT.
- No other FDG-avid cervical adenopathy.

Chest:
- Bilateral pulmonary nodules have continued to decrease. Dominant RLL nodule now measures approximately 1.3 cm with SUVmax 2.8 (mildly FDG-avid). LLL nodule 1.0 cm with SUVmax 2.1. RUL nodule 0.6 cm, not significantly FDG-avid. Other subcentimeter nodules without significant uptake.
- No new pulmonary lesions.
- No pleural or pericardial effusion.
- No mediastinal or hilar FDG-avid lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen:
- Liver: Three hepatic lesions are again identified. The dominant segment VI/VII lesion measures approximately 3.3 cm with SUVmax 5.8 (decreased from approximately 3.6 cm on recent CT). Segment IVa lesion measures 1.8 cm with SUVmax 4.2. Segment VIII lesion 1.3 cm with SUVmax 3.1. All three demonstrate decreased FDG avidity compared to what would be expected for untreated metastatic melanoma, suggesting partial metabolic response.
- No new hepatic lesions.
- Spleen, pancreas, adrenals, and kidneys: No FDG-avid lesions.
- No FDG-avid retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis:
- No FDG-avid pelvic disease.
- Mild diffuse prostatic uptake, likely physiological with BPH.

Musculoskeletal:
- No FDG-avid osseous lesions.
- Mild degenerative uptake in bilateral knees.

IMPRESSION:
1. Continued partial metabolic and anatomic response: Pulmonary nodules decreased in size with decreasing FDG avidity. Cervical lymphadenopathy decreased.
2. Hepatic metastases show mild anatomic decrease and importantly decreased FDG avidity (SUVmax 5.8 in dominant lesion), suggesting partial metabolic response despite prior stable appearance on CT alone.
3. No new sites of FDG-avid metastatic disease.
4. Overall: Continued partial response to immunotherapy.

CT CHEST, ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Metastatic melanoma on nivolumab monotherapy. 9-month restaging.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Axial images at 1.25 mm and 5 mm with reformats.

COMPARISON: PET/CT from approximately 3 months prior. CT CAP from approximately 4.5 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
- Pulmonary nodules remain stable to mildly decreased. RLL nodule 1.2 cm (previously 1.3 cm on PET/CT). LLL nodule 0.9 cm (previously 1.0 cm). RUL nodule 0.5 cm (previously 0.6 cm). Remaining subcentimeter nodules stable.
- No new pulmonary nodules.
- No pleural effusion.
- Right cervical adenopathy further decreased to 1.9 cm (previously 2.2 cm).

Abdomen:
- Liver: The dominant segment VI/VII lesion has INCREASED to 4.8 x 4.1 cm (previously 3.3 cm on PET/CT), with new areas of central necrosis and heterogeneous enhancement. Segment IVa lesion has increased to 2.9 cm (previously 1.8 cm). Segment VIII lesion has increased to 2.3 cm (previously 1.3 cm).
- NEW hepatic lesion identified in the left hepatic lobe (segment III), measuring 1.7 cm with peripheral enhancement.
- No ascites.
- Spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, kidneys: Unremarkable.
- No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis:
- Unremarkable. No new findings.

Musculoskeletal:
- No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. HEPATIC PROGRESSION: Significant interval growth of all three known hepatic metastases (dominant lesion now 4.8 cm, previously 3.3 cm) and a new left hepatic lobe (segment III) lesion measuring 1.7 cm. Findings represent unequivocal progressive disease in the liver.
2. Continued response in pulmonary metastases, which remain stable to mildly decreased.
3. Continued response in cervical lymphadenopathy.
4. Overall: Progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 based on hepatic progression, despite continued response at other sites (discordant response pattern).

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Restaging scans showing hepatic progression on nivolumab monotherapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 68-year-old male with Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma on nivolumab monotherapy (received 7 cycles of nivo 480 mg IV q4w following 2 cycles of ipi/nivo complicated by grade 3 colitis). Brain metastases treated with SRS remain well-controlled on recent MRI.

Restaging CT CAP obtained yesterday shows discordant response: continued improvement in pulmonary metastases and cervical lymphadenopathy, but unequivocal hepatic progression with growth of all three known liver lesions (dominant now 4.8 cm from 3.3 cm) and a new segment III lesion. This represents RECIST progressive disease.

LDH has risen to 410 U/L from a nadir of 342, concordant with hepatic progression.

The patient notes new right upper quadrant discomfort over the past 3 weeks, dull and aching, worse after meals. He has also noticed increased fatigue over the past month. No jaundice, no new skin lesions, no neurological symptoms. Weight stable.

Given BRAF V600E mutation and isolated hepatic progression with ongoing response at other sites, the recommended plan is to transition to BRAF/MEK targeted therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily. This approach leverages a different mechanism of action and BRAF-targeted agents have demonstrated hepatic response rates exceeding 60%.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Increased fatigue over past month, 5/10. No fevers, no night sweats.
GI: New RUQ discomfort as above. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Normal bowel function.
Skin: No rash, no new lesions.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal deficits.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 136/80, HR 78, Temp 98.4 F, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 190 lbs
General: Alert, oriented. Mild fatigue. ECOG PS 1.
HEENT: Scalp well-healed. No scleral icterus.
Neck: Right cervical adenopathy minimal, approximately 1.5 cm, soft.
Chest: Clear bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild tenderness in right upper quadrant on deep palpation. Liver edge palpable 4 cm below right costal margin, firm, non-nodular. No splenomegaly. No ascites.
Extremities: No edema.
Skin: Full-body exam negative for new melanocytic lesions.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 7.2 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, Platelets 232 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.08 mg/dL, BUN 20 mg/dL
AST 58 U/L (elevated), ALT 52 U/L (elevated), Alkaline phosphatase 168 U/L (elevated), Total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL
Albumin 3.4 g/dL
LDH 410 U/L (elevated, rising from 342 nadir)
TSH 2.18 mIU/L

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
68-year-old male with Stage IV M1d BRAF V600E melanoma with hepatic progression on nivolumab monotherapy despite continued extrahepatic response.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION: Unequivocal hepatic progression with growth of existing lesions and new segment III metastasis. Rising LDH and transaminases consistent with progressive hepatic disease burden.

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY CHANGE: Discontinue nivolumab. Initiate dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO daily. BRAF/MEK combination expected to yield rapid hepatic response. Prescriptions sent to specialty pharmacy.
   - Counseled patient on common side effects: pyrexia (dabrafenib), rash, fatigue, nausea, peripheral edema, diarrhea. Emphasized importance of reporting fevers >101.3 F immediately.
   - Dermatology referral for baseline skin exam and monitoring of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma risk with BRAF inhibitors.
   - Hold first 2 weeks of treatment and reassess tolerance before establishing routine follow-up cadence.

3. BRAIN METASTASES: Most recent MRI brain showed continued response to SRS. Dabrafenib/trametinib also has intracranial activity. Continue MRI brain surveillance every 3 months.

4. LIVER MONITORING: Baseline LFTs elevated (AST 58, ALT 52, ALP 168). Monitor LFTs every 2 weeks during first month of BRAF/MEK therapy, then monthly. Dose modify per package insert if significant hepatotoxicity.

5. ANTICOAGULATION: Continue apixaban 5 mg BID. No significant drug-drug interaction with dabrafenib/trametinib.

6. RESTAGING: CT CAP at 8 weeks on BRAF/MEK therapy. MRI brain at 12 weeks.

7. RETURN: 2 weeks for toxicity check and labs.

3. Cervical lymphadenopathy stable.
